sections.heading: Urgent Login Requests
points: Attackers exploit time pressure to disable rational thinking.
Common trigger phrases: 'Your account expires in 24 hours', 'Immediate action required'.
Legitimate services NEVER demand instant action through an unsolicited email.
Red flag combination: urgency + external link + unfamiliar sender domain.
Defence: navigate to the service directly — never via the email link.

sections.heading: Password Reset Scams
points: Fake reset emails mimic real services with near-identical logos and layouts.
Check the sender domain carefully — 'c0mpany.net' is NOT 'company.com'.
Hover over links before clicking: the destination URL reveals the truth.
If you did not request a reset, report the email to IT immediately.
Real IT departments NEVER ask for your password through any channel.

sections.heading: Verification Methods
points: Always verify by navigating to the official site in a new browser tab.
Call IT or HR directly using a number from the company directory — not the email.
Use multi-factor authentication: one compromised channel is not enough.
When in doubt, report and wait — the cost of checking is always zero.

sections.heading: Business Email Compromise (BEC)
points: BEC is the costliest cyber attack type globally — billions lost annually.
Attackers spoof executive email addresses to authorise fraudulent transfers.
Domain spoofing: 'ceo@company-secure-mail.net' is NOT 'ceo@company.com'.
Urgent wire transfer requests from leadership are a classic BEC indicator.
Finance teams: ALWAYS verify payment requests via a direct phone callback.

sections.heading: Authority Manipulation Tactics
points: Attackers impersonate CEOs, HR managers, IT staff, and external auditors.
They layer authority ('I am the CEO') with urgency ('NOW') and secrecy ('tell no one').
Requests for credentials, confidential data, or transfers are automatic red flags.
No legitimate executive will punish you for verifying an unusual request.
Social engineering exploits your deference to hierarchy — recognise that instinct.

sections.heading: Verification Workflows
points: Call back the executive at their known directory number — not a number in the email.
Escalate unusual requests to your manager before taking any action.
Use a two-person authorisation rule for all financial transfers above a set threshold.
Report suspicious emails to your security team — even if the sender looks legitimate.
Trust the process, not the pressure.

sections.heading: Fake Rewards & Prizes
points: Reward baiting exploits curiosity and desire for gain to bypass critical thinking.
Common lures: free certificates, gift cards, prize draws, exclusive offers.
Ask yourself: did I enter this competition? Does this reward match my role?
Scarcity tactics — 'limited time', 'only 10 left' — are designed to rush decisions.
Legitimate employer rewards are communicated through official channels, not posters.

sections.heading: Malicious QR Codes
points: QR codes cannot be read visually — you cannot see where they lead before scanning.
Attackers place fraudulent QR stickers over legitimate ones in public spaces.
A scanned QR code can open a phishing site, install malware, or pre-fill a payment.
Always check the full URL after scanning before tapping 'proceed'.
If the domain does not match the sponsoring organisation, do not proceed.

sections.heading: Scam Indicators
points: Unofficial domain: 'hr-certs.xyz' is not your company's domain.
Requests for login credentials or personal details to 'claim' a reward.
Pressure to act immediately before the offer expires.
Reward amount disproportionately large relative to the action required.
Report suspicious QR codes and posters to your IT or facilities team.

sections.heading: OTP Scams
points: Vishers call pretending to be IT support, banks, or government agencies.
They claim a security incident occurred and need your OTP to 'verify' you.
OTPs are single-use codes proving YOU initiated an action — sharing one grants full access.
No legitimate IT, bank, or authority EVER asks for an OTP by phone.
If asked for an OTP: hang up immediately and call the organisation directly.

sections.heading: Threat Manipulation
points: Fear is the strongest override of rational decision-making.
Threat phrases: 'Your account is compromised', 'Legal action will follow', 'You have 10 minutes'.
Attackers manufacture a crisis so that compliance feels like the only safe option.
Real emergencies give you time to verify — manufactured ones deliberately do not.
The more frightened a caller makes you feel, the more likely it is a scam.

sections.heading: Phone Verification
points: Caller ID is trivially spoofed — a call appearing from 'IT Dept' may not be.
Always hang up and call back using a number from the official company directory.
Ask the caller for a ticket number or reference ID — legitimate staff always have one.
Never give passwords, PINs, OTPs, or personal details on an inbound call.
Report all suspicious calls to your security team — your report protects colleagues.
